coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based composites as luminous down-conversion materials are becoming more popular due to several advantages such as steady fluorescence, ease of functionalization, tailoring of emission in the visible range, and so on. We report an inexpensive and environmentally sustainable synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen doped-CQDs produced from Cissus quadrangularis, a low-cost plant precursor with therapeutic value. The morphological, structural, and physicochemical features of the material were carefully investigated. Under UV stimulation (365 nm), almost spherical shaped N-CQDs with an average diameter of 5.1 nm were discovered to generate yellow-green fluorescence, have excellent photostability, and strong water solubility, with a quantum yield of up to 5 %. Furthermore, as a solid-phase dispersion matrix for CQDs, ecologically friendly and biodegradable bioplastic is appealing. The down-conversion of solid-state fluorescence of LEDs and UVC tubes was demonstrated by creating a nanocomposite by inserting N-CQDs into the solid matrix of a wheat starch-based bioplastic. Furthermore, employing constructed quantum dot-based optical displays, down-converted LEDs, and UVC tubes, the impacts of varied CQD concentrations and pH sensitivity were examined.

(e) SAED patterns of as-prepared N-CQDs. (f) X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX). (g) X-ray diffraction pattern of as-synthesized N-CQD.
(a) N-CQD XPS survey spectrum. (b) N-CQD C1s XPS spectra at high resolution. (c) N1s spectra at high resolution. (d) N-CQD O1s spectra.
(a) 13C and (b) 1H NMR spectra of as-synthesized N-doped CQDs derived from Cissus quadrangularis. pH dependent (c) FTIR spectra and (d) zeta potential.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-doped CQDs synthesised with and without EDA. The influence of (b) process temperature and (c) process time variation on the UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs. (d) pH dependent absorbance spectra of fabricated CQDs@bioplastic.

(a)-(c) CQDs manufactured both with and without EDA may be distinguished by their fluorescence spectra. Images of both samples subjected to natural light and UV (365 nm, 60 W) light are shown in the inset. N-CQD fluorescence spectra after hydrothermal treatment (d) temperature and (e) time changes. (d) Photographs of as-synthesized CQDs exposed to daylight and UV radiation at various process temperatures. A comparison of pH dependent (g) fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs and (h) normalized fluorescence spectra.
(a) Excitation dependent FL spectra of N-CQDs stimulated at various wavelengths. (b) The equivalent contour plot of excitation vs. emission wavelength.

(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Digital images of various concentrations of N-doped CQDs produced from Cissus quadrangularis spread in DI water under (a) daylight and (b) UV irradiation. N-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite was used to make a thin layer on a glass slide, and it was irradiated with (c) daylight and (d) UV light. When lighted with (e) daylight and (f) UV light, a quantum dot-based optical display with varying CQD concentrations casts a handwritten alphabet.

Thin film was created on a glass slide using a pH-dependent N-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite that was exposed to (c) daylight and (d) UV radiation. When lighted with (e) daylight and (f) UV light, a quantum dot-based optical display with varied pH-dependent CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite shows handwritten alphabet casting.

CQD concentration dependent emission (electroluminescent) spectra and related CIE chromaticity coordinates according to CIE 1931 of LEDs made by combining the UV-LED chip with N-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite phosphor produced from Cissus quadrangularis.

Ph sensitive emission (electroluminescent) spectra and related cie chromaticity co-ordinates of leds generated by combining the uv-led chip with n-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite based luminescent down-conversion material.

Digital photographs of commercially available UVC tube (254 nm, 11 W) (a) during OFF and ON condition, (b) wrapped with bioplastic film only, wrapped with (c) N-doped CQDs@ wheat starch bioplastic nanocomposite.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based composites as luminous down-conversion materials are becoming more popular due to several advantages such as steady fluorescence, ease of functionalization, tailoring of emission in the visible range, and so on. We report an inexpensive and environmentally sustainable synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen doped-CQDs produced from Cissus quadrangularis, a low-cost plant precursor with therapeutic value. The morphological, structural, and physicochemical features of the material were carefully investigated. Under UV stimulation (365 nm), almost spherical shaped N-CQDs with an average diameter of 5.1 nm were discovered to generate yellow-green fluorescence, have excellent photostability, and strong water solubility, with a quantum yield of up to 5 %. Furthermore, as a solid-phase dispersion matrix for CQDs, ecologically friendly and biodegradable bioplastic is appealing. The down-conversion of solid-state fluorescence of LEDs and UVC tubes was demonstrated by creating a nanocomposite by inserting N-CQDs into the solid matrix of a wheat starch-based bioplastic. Furthermore, employing constructed quantum dot-based optical displays, down-converted LEDs, and UVC tubes, the impacts of varied CQD concentrations and pH sensitivity were examined.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology is the key enabler in the twenty-first century, pushing the global economy toward a more eco-friendly and sustainable future. Light emitting diodes (LEDs) are a new generation green source of solid-state lighting that are extensively used in offices, hospitality, educational institutes as well as healthcare industry owing to their energy efficiency, low energy consumption, long lifespan, absence of radiation, and so on. LEDs are today's most energy-efficient and rapidly evolving lighting technology [1,2]. The increased usage of smart lighting systems is one of the primary factors driving the recent rise of the global LED lighting sector. LED lights use less energy and last longer than standard incandescent bulbs. As a consequence, LED lights are quickly replacing incandescent and fluorescent light sources. Indeed, the LED lighting industry is anticipated to be worth USD 124.7 billion by 2027, with a compound annual growth rate of 10.6 % [3,4] The wavelength down-conversion technique of semiconductor light generally employs UV-triggered down-conversion materials to produce visible light. However, it is critical to design and construct a simple, cost-effective, large-scale, ecologically friendly technology that employs low-cost beginning materials.

A new field of research known as “green synthesis of nanomaterials” has arisen to generate credible, sustainable, and ecologically benign materials. Since their unexpected discovery in early 2004, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have demonstrated to possess exceptional qualities, such as being environmentally friendly, straightforward to synthesise, non-other blinking, high biocompatibility, strong luminescence, durability, and high photostability, as well as extraordinary multi-photon excitation capability, ease of functionalization, and extraordinary water dispersibility [5–16]. Due to their many applications in fields such as bioimaging, biosensing, photonics, optoelectronics, and others, photonic nanomaterials based on carbon quantum dots are gaining popularity as viable alternatives to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots based on heavy metals [17–22].

Many rare-earth elements, organic dyes, inorganic based phosphors, and quantum dots are utilised as down conversion materials, including CdTe, CdSe/ZnSe, CuInS, CdSe/ZnS, CdSe/CdS/ZnS, CuInS2/ZnS, InP/ZnS, CdSe/ZnS QDs, and others [17,18,23–29]. While inorganic QD-based phosphors provide several advantages in terms of colorimetric flexibility, light output, and energy consumption, most of these QD phosphors contain hazardous materials such as Cd, Te, Se, and others. Furthermore, the cost-effectiveness of these phosphors in optoelectronics is limited since they are generated at high reaction temperatures with very costly precursors.

In this case, white or multicolored CQD-based LEDs make sense as a first option. There have been several reports of CQDs for multicolor or white LED down-conversion in the literature thus far, obtained from both organic and inorganic precursors [30–39]. Natural sources, such as herbal/medicinal herbs/plants, are particularly appealing in today's scenario for manufacturing diverse materials for agriculture and biomedical applications due to their biocompatibility and well-known natural ability. Producing carbon quantum dots from traditional herbal/medicinal herbs/plants, in particular, is a novel and forthcoming breakthrough with enormous promise in the field of biomedical diagnostics. The combination of traditional herbal/medicinal herbs/plant knowledge with nanotechnology may aid in resolving the complex healthcare difficulties currently confronting the global healthcare sector. Although there are numerous publications in the literature that use naturally derived CQDs for various biomedical applications, only a few articles use naturally derived CQDs for optoelectronics applications. In order to broaden the applications of medicinal herbs/plant-derived CQDs beyond biomedical applications, we demonstrate here the successful demonstration of N-CQDs derived from Cissus quadrangularis for future and cost-effective applications in quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion of LEDs. There has been no report in the literature that uses naturally derived CQDs for optoelectronics applications. To the best of our knowledge, no papers have been published on down-conversion LEDs produced from biomaterials of therapeutic plants. The reason behind selecting Cissus quadrangularis is that it is an important herbal supplement to treat conditions such as hemorrhoids, bone loss, allergies, asthma, and diabetes. The phytochemical compositions of Cissus quadrangularis are mainly consists of flavonoids, alkaloids, glycosides, steroids, saponins, tannins, triterpenes, benzenoids, phenols, etc. The stem parts of plant contains A and β-amyrins, β-sitosterol, ketosetosterol, phenols, tannins, vitamin, carotene, Calcium oxalate, 31 methyl tritiacontanoic acid, taraxeryl acetate, taraxeroliso-pentadecanoic acid, Calcium, phosphorus etc [40]. The presence of minerals in the extract is further beneficial for doping the synthesized nanoparticles.

Furthermore, the development of biodegradable polymers for industrial and commercial uses is imperative nowadays due to the substantial environmental effects associated with synthetic plastics. To the best of our knowledge, no research has been done on how bioplastic functions as a solid phase dispersion matrix and how it affects the photoluminescent properties of CQDs. The bioplastic was chosen not only for its ease of use, low cost, and environmental friendliness, but also to avoid self-quench in solid form owing to aggregation. Meanwhile, the starch matrix does not compete with excitation light for absorption and does not absorb CQD emissions, increasing fluorescence efficiency.

Therefore, the goal of this work is to develop an N-doped CQD synthesis from Cissus quadrangularis stem and leaf extract that is affordable, sustainable, and ecologically friendly. Additionally, biodegradable corn-starch-based bioplastic will be examined as a solid phase dispersion matrix of CQDs for possible uses in down-conversion light emitting diodes and quantum dot-based optical displays. In addition, the effects of different CQD concentrations and pH sensitivity were investigated using fabricated quantum dot-based optical displays and down-converted light-emitting diodes.

The stem and leaves of Cissus quadrangularis were obtained from a nearby university location. The chemicals and reagents used in this study were of research grade and were used as received without further purification. Besides, 0.22 µm syringe filter made of polytetrafluoroethylene membrane, Whatman filter paper (grade 1), 1,2-ethanediamine (EDA), ethanol, acetone, and isopropyl alcohol were also purchased from a local vendor. To acquire deionized water, a Milli-Q system (18.2 MΩ.cm, Millipore, France) was employed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green-synthesis of carbon quantum dots derived from Cissus quadrangularis
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were obtained from the extracts of plan parts (stem and leaves) of Cissus quadrangularis using a simple one-step hydrothermal green synthesis technique. In order to extract the juice, portions of stem and leaf were first cut into fine pieces and grinded in a home mixer. The ethylenediamine (EDA) solution (99 % pure) was then combined in a 3:7 (v/v) ratio with the fresh plant extract of the Cissus quadrangularis. The mixture was ultrasonically treated for approximately 10 min before being put into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Multiple iterations of this hydrothermal process were carried out at various temperatures (150–220 °C) and timeframes (1–12 Hrs). The centrifugation of the freshly created solution was done for 15 min at the speed of 9000–10,000 rpm when the solution was cooled to room temperature. The supernatant solution was recovered following centrifugation, and the pollutants settled. The supernatant solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm syringe filter to get rid of any contaminants that were still present after centrifugation (polytetrafluoroethylene membrane).

The filtered solution was then freeze-dried at −40 0C in a lyophilizer and stored for later use. The graphic representation of the entire procedure has been shown in Fig. 1 . The pH of the substance as it has been synthesised is about 4.17.

A REMI R8C or a CPR-30 Plus centrifuge was used for ultra-centrifugation. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were collected using a Molecular Devices SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer with a 1 nm step and wavelength ranges of 230–700 nm and 250–700 nm, respectively. The solution mixer was stirred using a REMI MS-500 magnetic stirrer. Using a Shimadzu IR Spirit attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transforms infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectrophotometer in the wavelength range 4000–400 cm−1, the surface functionalization components were examined. Physical Electronics' PHI 5000 Versa Probe III was used to measure the X-ray photoelectron spectra, which have a range of 0 to 1200 eV. A Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer at 40 kV, 40 mA, and a nonmonochromatic Cu Kα x-ray with an angular range of 5-90° and an angular step of 0.02° was used to determine the material's crystal structure. Utilizing a 500 MHz Bruker Advance Neo NMR spectrometer, the 13C- and 1H NMR spectroscopy experiments were conducted. It was determined what the surface Zeta potential was using the Anton Paar Litesizer 500. The SAED patterns and morphologies of synthesized CQDs were studied using a JEOL JEM 2100 Plus high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) with a 200 kV accelerating voltage. The relative concentrations of carbon and oxygen in the as-prepared CQDs were determined using the Energy-Dispersive x-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) of the JEOL JEM-2100F.

The relative quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized CQDs was calculated by applying the following formula:
φ =

, where x stands for CQDs, r for the reference dye (quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY: 0.54), I for the fluorescence emission intensity of the relevant system, A for optical absorption values, and for the material's refractive index value. Around 5 % QY was obtained. It is important to note that increased QY is still a problem for CQDs obtained from biomaterials as opposed to inorganic or synthetic sources. This is primarily because enhancing QY is still challenging when matched to employing simpler organic or inorganic resources, and plant extract containing a medicinally complex mixture of various plant metabolites, including alkaloids, terpenoids, phenolics, minerals, glycosides, vitamins, and flavonoids. Supplemental Table S1 makes use of information from the literature to compare the quantum yield of CQDs derived from a variety of plants of medicinal value and herbs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of down-conversion luminescent bioplastic nanocomposites for applications in optical displays, LEDs, UVC-tubes
ce:section.ce:para: First and foremost, an eco-friendly bioplastic-based quantum dot-based optical display prototype was established. Commercially available wheat starch (popularly known as maida), vinegar, and glycerine were used to synthesize the bioplastic. Vinegar and glycerine were combined in a 1:1 vol ratio with 0.2 gm of wheat starch and stirred on a hotplate at 50 °C for around 10–15 min. Following that, dropwise additions of varied concentrations (5, 10, 20, and 80 μL/mL) of as-synthesized solution of N-doped CQDs were added mixed well and stirred for an additional 10–15 min. Prior to mixing, the CQD solution was well sonicated for 5–10 min in an ultrasonic bath. Using a doctored blade approach, the as-prepared CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite was subsequently coating was done on a glass slide. Similar casting techniques were followed with CQD solutions of varied pH values (pH: 2.54, 4.17, 7.5 and 11). However, to form the letters, the as-prepared CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite was placed in a plastic cone and slightly squeezed. The designed casting was then cured at room temperature for 24 h. The mixture was placed either in a prefabricated dome-shaped plastic mould that held a commercial ultraviolet (UV-C) LED (275 nm, 0.5 W) or wrapped over a commercial UVC-tube (254 nm, 11 W) and left to rest at room temperature for 24–48 h to fabricate the N-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite luminescent materials for LEDs and UVC-tubes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of Cissus quadrangularis derived-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphology of as-synthesized N-CQDs were examined by HR-TEM. Fig. 2 (a)-(c) show the TEM images at different magnifying scales and corresponding d-spacing. As demonstrated in figures the particle size is generally dispersed between 4 and 9 nm, with an average diameter of 5.1 nm, indicating the nanoscale of CQDs for their quantum confinement effect as shown in Fig. 2(d). The materials are tiny, homogeneous, and almost spherical in shape, as observed in TEM micrographs. Moreover, the weak poly-crystalline structure of the as-prepared N-CQDs was shown by the broad diffused ring in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shown in Fig. 2(e). Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), on the other hand, was employed to investigate the elemental compositions of the as-synthesized N-CQDs. The existence of different functionalization groups (such as hydroxyl, amine, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups) on the surface of the manufactured N-CQDs is indicated by high carbon (48.37 wt%), nitrogen (10.39 wt%), and oxygen (41.24 wt%) concentrations in energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy measurements (Fig. 2(f)). The enhanced oxygen percentage in the N-CQD EDX spectrum is most likely caused by the oxygen groups seen in the functional groups. Furthermore, XRD validated the crystallinity of the as-prepared N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(g).

The overall poor crystal quality is identified by the XRD pattern, which has a broad diffraction peak at a central angle of 23.8° due to the (002) plane (JCPDS No. 26–1076). This is caused by the turbostratic structure that comes from the interlayer packing of disordered carbon atoms in the hexagonal graphite structure of CQDs [41–44]. The amorphous shape of the produced particles had an impact on the wide peak as well. A tiny peak at 42.1° also suggests graphitization along the (100) plane. The interlayer distance (d-spacing) for the (002) plane was determined to be 0.37 nm and 0.21 nm for the (100) plane.

Furthermore, the chemical composition and functional groups of the N-doped CQDs were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum as shown in Fig. 3 (a) exhibits a visible graphitic C1s peak at roughly 284.5 eV, an O1s peak at 529.9 eV and a distinct N1s peak at 398.8 eV.

Each of the three components of the C1s signal had a centre energy of 282.7 eV, 283.9 eV, and 285.9 eV, corresponding to the sp2C-sp2C, N-sp2C, and C-OH bonds, respectively (see Fig. 3(b)) [45–47]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. 3(c)) can be deconvoluted into three independent peaks, each centred on 397.2 eV, 397.7 eV, and 398.8 eV, emanating from pyrrolic/pyridinic-N, quaternary-N (N-C3), and O = C-N, demonstrating that nitrogen atoms were efficiently doped in CQDs aside from the notable C-N Additionally, the presence of the C = O and C-OH/C-O-C groups can be shown by splitting the O1s spectra in Fig. 3(d) into two components, 528.8 eV and 529.9 eV [48,49]. According to calculations, the atomic percentages of C, N, and O are roughly 59.66, 8.14, and 32.2, respectively, which is in agreement with EDX spectrum data. The interaction between the Cissus quadrangularis extract and the ethylenediamine functionalized the surface of the CQDs as-produced with a range of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups, according to the XPS data.

Aside from that, the sp3- and sp2- composition of the core was investigated using Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. Protons and carbons of sp3- and sp2- hybridized carbon atoms were identified using the 1H and 13C NMR of synthesized CQDs. Tetramethyl silane (TMS) was used as a reference for the chemical shifts in terms of ppm. In Fig. 4 (a), the 1H NMR spectrum is displayed. According to the chemical shift, there are four main regions that can be roughly classified as follows: 1–3 ppm (protons of sp3 hybridized carbon), 3–6 ppm (protons of hydroxyl, ether, and carbonyl groups), 6–8 ppm (for aromatic protons), and 8–10 ppm (for aromatic and aldehydic protons) [50].

Again, based on 13C NMR spectra, Fig. 4(b) displays a mixture of carbon atoms that have undergone sp3 and sp2 hybridization. Four categories can be used to categorise the regions: 20–80 ppm for sp3 carbons and carbons linked to hydroxyl and amine groups, 80–100 ppm for carbons connected to ether linkages, 100–120 ppm for C = C aromatic or sp2 hybridised carbon atoms, and 175–190 ppm for C = O carbons. Therefore, the NMR results show that the synthesized CQDs consist of a mixture of sp3 and sp2- hybridized carbon atoms, while sp3-hybridized carbon atoms dominate.

pH, on the other hand, has a significant influence on the optical characteristics of CQDs. It has previously been reported that pH sensitivity can emerge as a result of a variety of events, including the formation of novel functional group in the shells [51,52]. These processes include changes in energy levels, protonation and deprotonation, aggregation, protective shell or proton transfer mechanism. In fact, FTIR analyses have revealed the existence of several nitrogen and oxygen functional groups and linkages in carbon quantum dots treated to various pH settings (ranging from 2.53 to 11.00), as shown in Fig. 4(c). The presence of –OH, -C-H, C = O, N-H, and C-N, which correspond to the stretching frequencies at 3334.39, 2952.51, 1624.45, 1356.56, and 923.37 cm−1, respectively, was easily detected in all samples [53]. But when the pH was changed from neutral (pH: 7.53) to acidic, the intensities of two bonds connected with -N = C = O at 2329.4 cm−1 and -N = C = N- at 2013.37 cm−1 significantly enhanced (pH: 2.53). It is anticipated that these hydrophilic functional groups will significantly boost the generated carbon quantum dots' water solubility.

In addition, the synthesised fluorescent CQDs' surface charge was measured using a zetasizer in order to determine the zeta potential—also referred to as the electro kinetic potential—and how it affected pH fluctuations. The features of CQDs, such as their double layer structure and diversity of hydrophilic or hydrophobic functional groups, are further clarified by this approach. The surface charge of nanoparticles in solution determines their zeta potential. It is a useful method for determining the surface state of nanoparticles and the long-term durability of a colloidal dispersion. The pH of a sample has a significant impact on its zeta potential in aqueous media. As seen in Fig. 4(d), it has been shown that zeta potential does, in fact, alter with pH. When pH is more acidic, there is a noticeable shift in the positive directions. This suggests that additional positive charge is developing as a result of the reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups like carboxylic and amide groups [54,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of Cissus quadrangularis derived-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the optical absorption peak of the as-synthesised CQDs without the addition of EDA was found in the UV region, with a maximum absorption starting at about 284.8 nm and a tail extending into the range of 366.1 nm. This is because of the normal n-π* transitions in the graphene-like structure of the core and the π-π* transition in the conjugated C = C band [46]. However, the highest absorption peak for the EDA-added as-synthesized CQDs was discovered to be relocated to 380.7 nm, while the second absorption peak was discovered to be located at 476.6 nm, as shown in Fig. 5(a). The presence of functional groups near the core's edge is thought to be responsible for the second absorption peak. Furthermore, these absorption transitions between the core and edge states in as-synthesised CQDs result in hole-electron excitons in aqueous environments. Then, the effects of process variables such as time and temperature on CQDs generated by EDA were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 5(b), both absorption peaks shifted to longer wavelengths for process temperatures of 180 °C and 200 °C compared to the other two, 436.3 nm for the 150 °C process and 459.4 nm for the 220 °C process temperature. The significant spectral shift in the absorption spectra, especially at higher temperatures and longer interaction time between EDA and precursor, is anticipated due to additional functional groups that are introduced by process variation as well different nucleation process which results into different particle size distributions. Meanwhile, when the process temperature was kept constant, but the same pattern was seen when the process duration was varied (Fig. 5(c)). The spectral shift are typically depending on temperature, nucleation, particle size, and functional groups. The increased conversion of components to carbonization during the longer hydrothermal reaction period is anticipated to induce agglomeration of particles following nucleation to last longer, influencing the growth of larger particles and hence causing the observed blue shift in Fig. 5(c).
As a consequence, based on consistency and repeatability, as well as UV–Vis and fluorescence characteristics, the hydrothermal process parameters were optimized at 180 °C for 3 h.

Additionally, the hydrothermal process temperatures ranged from 150 to 220 °C, and the traditional Tauc plot was utilised to evaluate the fluctuation in optical energy band gap of the produced CQDs [56–59]. The estimated optical energy band gap is in the range of 2.4–3.06 eV, as shown in Table S2 in the supplemental data (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, the pH-sensitive absorption behaviour of the prepared CQDs@bioplastic was also investigated. According to the relationship between pH and absorbance, a decrease in pH caused a decrease in absorbance in the UV–Vis spectrum, which may be due to the aromatic chemicals present in the bioplastic (see Fig. 5(d)).

Contrarily, carbon quantum dots exhibit size-dependent fluorescence activity and an emission wavelength. Fig. 6 (a) displays the fluorescence (FL) spectra of CQDs. At the same concentration of CQDs dispersed in aqueous solutions (1 mg/mL), the fluorescence emission from CQDs synthesised with EDA was about 1.8 times greater than that from CQDs synthesised without EDA. These results suggest that nitrogen doping enhances fluorescence emission properties. Similar to this, after stimulation at 260 nm, the emission peak ranges from 320 to 610 nm. Three distinct broad peaks were visible in the deconvoluted fluorescence spectra as shown in Fig. 6(b)-(c), with maximum emission peaks at 386, 474, and 515 nm with percentage contributions of 18.72 %, 44.94 %, and 36.32 %, respectively. This indicates that greenish fluorescent emission predominates. In daylight, both CQD aqueous solutions were pale yellow and clear, but when subjected to UV light (365 nm, 60 W), they fluoresced brightly and intensely green, as seen in the inset of Fig. 6(a). In the interim, it has been investigated how hydrothermal process temperature and time affect fluorescence emission from CQDs with EDA. The greatest emission peaks remained fairly constant around 480 nm as the processing temperature rose, with the highest peak changing to 390 nm for the sample processed at 220 °C. The emission spectra widened as processing temperature climbed (see Fig. 6(d)). Fig. 6(d) shows images of an aqueous solution of N-CQDs made at different temperatures and exposed to visible light and a 365 nm UV lamp. However, when the process length was changed at a certain process temperature (180 °C), as shown in Fig. 6(e), no appreciable change in peak maxima was observed. Fluorescence emission that is dependent on the procedure is summarised in Table S1 and Fig. S2. The digital photographs of the samples under daylight and UV are shown in Fig. 6(f). Similarly, Fig. 6(g) and 6(h) show a comparison of pH dependent fluorescence spectra and normalised spectra. The overall FL intensity was found to increase (14 % for pH: 2.53, 29.7 % for pH: 7.4, and 11.6 % for pH:11.0) with pH change when compared to the as-synthesized one. The corresponding deconvolution spectra are shown in Fig. S3.

It is noteworthy that CQDs often exhibits excitation dependent fluorescence. Hence, excitation dependent FL spectra were investigated in this work. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. 7 (a) show that the excitation wavelength of CQDs is 400 nm, while the emission wavelength is 491 nm. The emission wavelength increased from 348 to 527 nm when the excitation wavelength was increased from 250 to 445 nm. According to the findings, photon reabsorption shows excitation-dependent fluorescence, which is connected to the quantum confinement effect and edge defects. Fluorescence emission peaks move towards redshift as the excitation wavelength rises. Color-coded maps of photoemission are shown in Fig. 7(b), with emission wavelength on the y-axis, excitation on the x-axis, and the colour scale representing FL intensity.

However, using the excitation-dependent FL spectra, a down-conversion strategy for coloured LEDs can be developed and implemented. As shown in Fig. 8 (a) and (b), CIE (Commission International d'Eclairage) chromaticity coordinates, colour rendering index (CRI), and correlated colour temperature (CCT) were computed in accordance with CIE 1931 and CIE 1976 [60–62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed fluorescence mechanism of Cissus quadrangularis derived N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots frequently display changing photoluminescence and wavelength-dependent emission. Fluorescence color is often ascribed to electronic bandgap fluctuations in conjugated -domains, surface defect states, local fluorophores, and element doping (with nitrogen, sulphur, other heteroatoms, and metal ions). In this work, a scheme of CQD energy band structure is provided based on the XPS, FTIR, and fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs made from Cissus quadrangularis, as shown in Fig. 9 . High levels of particle diversity may be seen in the excitation dependent FL emission that has been seen.

These emissions unmistakably demonstrate that it is composed of a range of chromophoric band-gap structures produced by C and N-related groups, including C-C/C = C groups (intrinsic-C), C-N (graphitic-N), N-containing heterocycles (pyridine N), and C = O groups or grain boundaries [45]. A distinct border zone or spherical shell that divides the carbon core region or body from the surface is referred to as a surface defect. The spheroidal region is composed of numerous chemical groups formed from sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons, additional surface functional groups, and dangling bonds. Surface oxidation is the most common cause of surface defects. Surface defects can serve as exciton capture sites, resulting in surface defect state fluorescence. As shown schematically in Fig. 9(a), carbon quantum dots have a core composed of many-layered graphitic carbon sheets, graphitic nitrogen, and a shell composed of hydroxyl (–OH) and amine (–NH2) functional groups. The optical band-gap structure of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 9(a) and (b) under UV (295 nm) and visible blue light (400 nm) illuminations. The localised -electron in double bonds (typically C = C or C = N) absorbs UV photons under 295 nm UV stimulation, causing the electron to transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the carbon core, H3 → L3, to an exciton. This exciton may produce UV light via radiative recombination after vibration relaxation via route-I (L3 → H3). While the excited electron can produce visible light via pathways II (L2 → H2) or III (L1 → H1), it can also make an inter-band transition from a higher to a lower LUMO via L3 → L2 or L3 → L1. Conjugated π-electrons are able to transmit excitons and produce visible light via path-I' after absorbing blue photons in response to 400 nm visual stimulation. Additionally, it may choose the route-II' (L1 → H1) during vibration relaxation. This excitation results in photons with emission wavelengths that are approximately 490 nm longer than the absorbed wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 9(b) for 400 nm. The nitrogen groups found on CQDs may be divided into four categories based on the XPS observations: amino, pyrrole, pyridine, and graphite. CQDs have an amino N on their surface, pyrrole N and pyridine N at the edge and core of the structure, respectively, and graphite N on the core. The fluorescence potential of CQDs is further highlighted by the clear nitrogen structure and dispersion. Amino groups are thought to increase the fluorescence of CQDs by self-passivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of Cissus quadrangularis derived N-CQDs for quantum dot-based optical displays, LEDs and UVC tube down-conversion
ce:section.ce:para: As previously mentioned, numerous studies have been done on the use of synthetic polymers for down-conversion LED applications, including epoxy resin, PVK (poly-N-vinyl carbazole), silicone resin, and PMMA (poly-methyl methacrylate) to embed CQDs in a solid-state matrix. However, the use of sustainable materials is still relatively low. The aim of this research is to present a working LED prototype and quantum dot optical display using bioplastic made from wheat starch. In this regard, Table S3 summarizes a literature survey on the various materials that have been used to fabricate down-converted LEDs using CQDs. It is worth noting that lanthanide-doped nanoparticles, a traditional group of luminescence phosphors, frequently demonstrate promising photostability, near-infrared (NIR)-excited frequency up-/down-converting capabilities, emission fine-tuning, and multispectral features. They have therefore been effectively applied in several optoelectronics applications. Herein, a comparison of various phosphors and luminescent nanomaterials that have been utilized in down conversion of LEDs are discussed in Table S4 and S5.

As previously mentioned in the experimental part, a bioplastic based on wheat starch was used to create the quantum dot-based optical display prototype and LEDs in this work. In addition to being easy to use, inexpensive and environmentally friendly, the CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite was also chosen to avoid aggregation-induced solid-state quenching. The starch matrix also does not compete with the excitation light for absorption and does not absorb the CQD emission, thus improving the fluorescence efficiency.

The first research looked into how CQD concentrations affected optical displays. Digital pictures of as-synthesized N-doped CQDs at various concentrations (5, 10, 20, and 80 μL/mL) under natural light and UV radiation are shown in Fig. 10 (a)-(b) (365 nm, 60 W). The colour of the emission varies from vivid green to dark yellowish green. Similar patterns for the CQD@bioplastic nanocomposite thin film and letter casting on glass slides are shown in Fig. 10(c)–10(f).

Optical displays made of N-doped CQDs at various pH levels, on the other hand, have been investigated. When exposed to UV light, the colour of the manufactured optical display, whether thin film or letter casting, changes from deep green to yellowish green when the pH value increases from acid (pH: 2.54) to base (pH: 11), as shown in Fig. 11 (a)-(f).

Additionally, down-conversion LEDs were created using N-doped CQDs@bioplastic; the specifics of which may be found in the experiment section. First, it was investigated how CQD concentration affected LED conversion. As the concentration of CQDs increases from 10 μL/mL to 80 μL/mL (yellowish-green), the emission color of the converted UV-LED shifts towards higher wavelengths, as seen in Fig. 12 . Furthermore, the luminosity and efficiency of the converted UV LED were evaluated. The electroluminescent (EL) spectrum of the converted LED at a forward bias current of 15 mA is shown in Fig. 12(b) for samples containing CQDs@bioplastic at different concentrations (10, 20, and 80 μL/mL). The chromatic coordinates as determined from the different concentrated samples are (0.29, 0.55), (0.39, 0.56), and (0.31, 0.57) for 10 μL/mL, 20 μL/mL, and 80 μL/mL, respectively, according to the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage, (CIE-1931).

In addition, the converted LEDs' pH-sensitive electroluminescence characteristics were researched, as depicted in Fig. 13 . In Fig. 13 (P2c) and (P4c), the effect of pH is evident. As the pH value rises from acid (pH: 2.53) to base (pH: 11), the chromatic coordinates according to CIE-1931 change slightly from (0.33, 0.58) to (0.35, 0.58).

In light of this, these prototypes show how N-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite may be utilised to provide a quick and inexpensive method of down converting UV LED chips into yellowish-green LEDs. However, future studies might concentrate on the critical optical properties of CQDs@bioplastic-based down-conversion LEDs (such CIE and efficiency).

Furthermore, for large-scale applications of the fabricated ecologically sustainable bioplastic nanocomposite, a commercial UVC tube (254 nm, 11 W) commonly used for bacterial disinfection or RO water purifier has been chosen to extend its capabilities as a visible tube light beyond its normal usage. Fig. 14 (a) shows a digital photograph of a commercial UVC tube emitting ultraviolet radiation. But, no significant changes in emission colour were observed when the tube was simply wrapped in wheat starch-based bioplastic, as shown in Fig. 14(b). Therefore, the bioplastic film alone is transparent in the ultraviolet range. However, when the tube was wrapped with bioplastic nanocomposite, an intense green-yellow colour (CIE co-ordinates: 0.37, 0.68) was emitted as seen in Fig. 14(c). This example clearly demonstrates how N-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite may be efficiently used for the down-conversion of UV LEDs and UVC tubes. However, future research might focus on the fundamental optical features (such as CIE and efficiency) of CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite.

In summary, yellow-green fluorescence emitting carbon quantum dots were synthesized from a plant extract of Cissus quadrangularis, a traditional medicinal plant, using a simple, cost-effective, eco-friendly, and sustainable synthesis technique. CQDs have been found to be 4–9 nm in size (average size 5.1 nm), with a quantum yield of roughly 5 %, yellow-greenish fluorescent in nature, with excellent water solubility and photo-stability. The composition, crystallinity, and functional groups of as-synthesized N-doped CQDs were evaluated by XRD, FTIR and XPS studies. The presence of sp3 and sp2 carbons was confirmed using 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectroscopy. In addition, the mechanism of potential fluorescence is investigated. However, because of the severe environmental consequences of synthetic plastics, the development of biodegradable polymers for industrial and commercial applications is crucial today. Thus, N-doped CQDs embedded in wheat-starch-based bioplastic nanocomposite thin film were created as a solid phase dispersion matrix of CQDs to show down-conversion of LEDs, UVC tubes, and optical displays. The effects of different CQD doses and pH sensitivity were also investigated. As indicated by the chromatic coordinates computed using the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE-1931), a rise in either CQD concentration of a certain pH value or pH-varied CQDs in bioplastic nanocomposite shifted the electroluminescence colour towards a deeper one. The chromatic coordinates according to CIE-1931 alter somewhat when the pH value rises from acid (pH: 2.53) to base (pH: 11). When the tube was covered with bioplastic nanocomposite, however, a bright green-yellow colour was emitted (CIE co-ordinates: 0.37, 0.68). Therefore, this work demonstrates the potential of N-doped CQD@bioplastic nanocomposites for effective luminous down-conversion of UV-LED and UVC tubes in the future.
